section_5_core_concepts.detail: Five subsections (5.1–5.5) present with substantial elaboration: etymology + history, defined steps, computability, non-electronic examples, AI connection.

section 5.2: defined steps + recipe analogy [1] (multiple locations)
section 5.3: table rows for all computable examples [1]

section 5.3: why the boundary matters paragraph [1][2]

factual_assertion_coverage.methodology: Paragraph-closing and inline [N] markers counted as covering the factual assertions in that block. Section 6 is instructional/exercise content with no standalone externally-sourced factual claims.

uncited_assertions_residual: Section 5.1 numbered list (lines 47–49): I/P/O definitions — follow directly from the cited definition block immediately above; no inline [N] on each bullet. Low severity.
Section 5.1 line 51: 'This input→process→output pattern is so fundamental' — transitional framing sentence, no [N]. Low severity.
Section 5.2 line 67: '...why languages like pseudocode and flowcharts exist' — reasoned inference, no [N]. Low severity.
Section 5.3 table lines for 'poem not computable' and 'weather not computable' — illustrative definitional examples, no [N]. Low severity.
Section 5.4 line 98: 'The word computer today almost always means an electronic device' — common knowledge framing, no [N]. Low severity.

compliant_mentions: 'deterministic' / 'probabilistic' (section 5.5 line 144): named with explicit topic pointers — 'you will explore deterministic systems and probabilistic systems in topics 1.2 and 1.3.' Mention-and-defer compliant (≤1 sentence + deferral).
'pseudocode' and 'flowcharts' (section 5.2 line 67): named with module-level deferral — 'you will cover those in later topics in this module.' Mention-and-defer compliant.
'AI (Artificial Intelligence)' (section 5.3 line 90): spelled out in parentheses on first use. Used as motivational application context, not taught as standalone concept. Compliant.

'feedback loop' (section 5.4 thermostat): labels the described observable behaviour in one sentence ('This loop — output affecting future input — is called a feedback loop'). Not taught at depth; no prior topic defines it. Illustrative-label compliant.
'algorithm': prior hard forward-reference confirmed removed — table row now reads 'A set of defined steps exists' (verified in current text). RESOLVED.
'trained model': prior hard forward-reference confirmed removed — manufacturing example now reads 'a set of rules that checks...' (verified in current text). RESOLVED.
'Excalidraw' (section 9): proper noun tool name, not a CS concept. Excluded from sequence-drift check.

length_sanity.detail: Corpus is approximately 3300 words. The standard-profile range is 3000–4400 words. The corpus sits approximately 10% above the lower bound and 25% below the upper bound — well within the canonical range. Prior failure at ~1584 words has been resolved. The expansion covers: five fully elaborated subsections in section 5 (etymology, defined steps, computability table, five non-electronic device walk-throughs, AI connection), an Implementation thought-experiment in section 6, three rich domain patterns in section 7 (manufacturing, finance, healthcare), and a Best Practices section 8.

compliant_mentions: Section 5.5 'Why this matters for AI': frames AI as an instance of computation (I/P/O). States learning about AI mechanism is deferred to a later module. Does not teach how AI learning or inference works. Motivational bridge only. Compliant.
'feedback loop' label (section 5.4 thermostat): one sentence, labels observable behaviour. No teaching of control-theory or feedback-control systems. Compliant.
'deterministic' / 'probabilistic' appearances (sections 5.4, 5.5, 7, 8): used as descriptive labels with explicit deferrals to topics 1.2/1.3. Not taught as standalone concepts. Compliant.

Section 7 real-world patterns (manufacturing, finance, healthcare): all three examples are structured to illustrate input→process→output at scale. They do not introduce concepts beyond the topic headline — they apply and reinforce it across domains. Compliant.
Prior hard scope_creep finding ('(or a trained model)' in manufacturing): confirmed removed from current text. RESOLVED.

Prior soft scope_creep finding ('applies learned patterns from thousands of previous scans' in healthcare): confirmed replaced with 'applies a set of rules built from many previous examples'. RESOLVED.

findings.detail: 'Deterministic' is used at section 5.4 ('The process is deterministic') before the explicit mention-and-defer in section 5.5. However, each use is immediately preceded by a plain-language description of the behaviour ('same elapsed-time input always produces the same phase output'), so a beginner can infer the meaning from context. Not a cold usage. Soft finding only.

'I/P/O' abbreviation first appears at section 5.1 ('The I/P/O model') two sentences after the full terms Input / Process / Output were defined in the numbered list immediately above. Low severity — the full expansion is present in the same paragraph. Soft finding only.

beginner_readability.escalation: 2 distinct findings — below the ≥3 threshold for hard-fail escalation. No escalation.

fixes_confirmed.resolution: Term removed from table row in section 5.3 (replaced with 'A set of defined steps exists'). Prior soft finding RESOLVED.
Parenthetical removed from manufacturing example in section 7. Prior soft finding RESOLVED.
